title: Disembodiment: corporeal politics of radical refusal [Book Review]
author: Martel

opening_move.description: The review opens with enthusiastic endorsement of Bargu's Disembodiment as a timely and brilliant work on the relationship between bodily subjectivity, domination, and resistance — framing it as a dialectical history of body and the subject that structures colonialism, slavery, and capitalism.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The political ontology of the body is the ground for resistance that domination can never fully extinguish — bodily materiality as the limit of sovereign power

theoretical_positioning.description: Appreciative and expository review of Bargu's theory of corporeal resistance. The reviewer contextualises Bargu's contribution relative to James Scott and the Frankfurt School, extending the book's argument rather than critiquing it.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Bargu; engagement with critical theory (Horkheimer and Adorno); analysis of film (Hunger by Steve McQueen)
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review (3 pages). Primarily expository and enthusiastic. The Hunger film and Odysseus parable serve as the key interpretive cases within Bargu's argument as presented by the reviewer.

case_type.case_name: Bargu, Disembodiment: Corporeal Politics of Radical Refusal (2024); film: Hunger (Steve McQueen)

confidence_notes: Book review (3 pages). The film Hunger is discussed as an interpretive case within Bargu's argument (not independently by the reviewer). Classification as normative_with_interpretive_case reflects both the film analysis and the normative claim about bodily resistance.
